job_description:
Job Description

Quaker Houghton invites you to apply for an internship located in Conshohocken, PA. This internship is designed to provide you with a positive work/training experience and challenging objectives.

As a Software Developer Intern for QH Fluid Intelligence and R&D Platforms, you’ll be part of our diverse global team making a significant impact on our organization. You will use your analytical thinking skills combined with your experience in applying design patterns and best practices, structured and disciplined development processes and engineering principles to developing robust, scalable and maintainable solutions that are user friendly and support business priorities.

Our FI products are installed and used by factories that uses metals, forms and deforms metals by forging, forming, cutting, fabrication including automobiles to beverage cans that you use every day. You will analyze, design, program, debug, modify, test and implement software used in local, networked, cloud-based IoT Devices and connected applications for Fluid Intelligence.

How you will do it:

 This will be a full-time Summer 2026 internship running from May to August
 Hybrid position – at least 3 days a week in Conshohocken PA office
 Tasked with a specific project In Fluid Intelligence or R&D Software Applications
 Assist with Software Development using Python, C/C++, JavaScript, IoT connectivity, Data analysis and exploration etc. 
 Participate in the Future Leaders Internship Programming events that include but are not limited to professional development sessions, 4-week Innovation Challenge and special events

What we look for

Required Qualifications:

 Currently enrolled as a full-time student at an accredited U.S. college or university
 Pursuing a degree in Computer Science, Software Engineering/Design
 U.S. citizen or legal right to work in the U.S. 

Preferred Qualifications:

 Demonstrates an ability to learn quickly and independently
 Strong communication skills (verbal and written) preferred
 Demonstrated problem solving skills required
resume:
Berkeley, CA | (555) 123-4567 |  youremail@berkeley.edu  | Linkedin | website, and/or portfolio URL (optional)

Education
University of California, Berkeley	                                                                                                                                                                      May 2026         
Bachelor of Arts in Data Science	                                                                                                                                               GPA: ___/4.0 (optional)

Relevant Coursework: Data Structures & Algorithms, Modern Statistical Prediction & Machine Learning, Principles & Techniques of Data Science, Data, Inference, & Decisions, and Human Contexts & Ethics of Data


Skills
Languages: SQL, Python, R, Java
Databases: MySQL, SQLite
Machine Learning: Scikit-learn, SAS
Tools: Pandas, Matplotlib, Tableau, Excel, BeautifulSoup

Highlighted Experience and Projects
Data Analyst Intern, ABC Company, San Francisco, CA					    	      	   Summer 2025      
Analyzed user engagement data using SQL and Python (Pandas, Matplotlib) to identify trends in app usage, providing insights that supported a 15% increase in user acquisition for high-value user segments
Developed and implemented data cleaning procedures (Python) for a customer satisfaction survey dataset, leading to a 10% reduction in data processing time for future surveys
Collaborated with software engineers to understand data storage structures and API functionalities, ensuring accurate data retrieval for analysis


Datathon Project: Predicting Customer Churn for XYZ Company                                                                           	               Spring 2025
Led a team of 4 in the exploration of customer data, uncovering key trends and patterns related to churn
Guided team in selecting machine learning algorithms using Scikit-learn for churn prediction; oversaw model training, evaluation, and hyperparameter tuning to optimize performance
Prepared and delivered a final presentation to datathon judges, highlighting key findings and model performance (78% accuracy on unseen test data)


Project: Fantasy Football Modeling, Course: Data and Decisions                                                                       	                             Fall 2024  
Aggregated and prepped 5 years of NFL fantasy football projection data from 6 independent sources into a MySQL database
Built a random forest model in SAS that improved projection accuracy by combining the disparate sources into one projection that outperformed the mean absolute error of the next best projection by 18%


Project: Production Control, Course:  System and Analysis Design	                                                                                          Spring 2024
Led a team of five students in designing, coding, and implementing a SQL database
Entered and updated information using the web scraping tool BeautifulSoup
Completed analysis and designed documentation with data flow diagrams, structural charts, process specifications, a data dictionary, and a user manual


Leadership and Extracurricular Activities
Data Structures Undergraduate Student Instructor, UC Berkeley EECS Dept	                                       Sept. 2024 - May 2025
Supported biweekly sections of 100+ students to help reinforce core data structures concepts (e.g. asymptotics, linked lists, trees, searching/sorting algorithms, etc.)
Shaped course curriculum by developing relevant enrichment problems to help students master concepts

CS Educator / Events Committee Member, Berkeley ANova	                                                                             Sept. 2023 - May 2024
Improved computer science education in under-resourced communities across the Bay Area
Taught a weekly project-based after-school program at Bay Area middle schools
weak_topic_memory: describing a more complex project scenario

history.question: Tell me about a time when you had to collaborate with a team to solve a complex problem, such as the Datathon Project where you led a team to predict customer churn. How did you ensure that your team was working effectively together, and what was the outcome of your collaboration?

Describe a situation where you had to communicate complex technical ideas to a non-technical audience, such as explaining your data analysis findings to stakeholders or presenting your project results to a panel. How did you approach the communication, and what strategies did you use to ensure your message was understood?
history.feedback: The candidate did not provide a response to the question, which is a critical aspect of a behavioral interview. To effectively communicate complex technical ideas, it's essential to provide a specific example from past experience.
history.critique: The candidate failed to provide a concrete example, making it impossible to assess their ability to communicate technical ideas to a non-technical audience.
history.rewrite: In my previous role, I had to present data analysis findings to a stakeholder group with limited technical background. I approached the communication by first understanding the stakeholders' needs and concerns, and then tailoring my message to focus on the key insights and recommendations. I used analogies, visual aids, and simple language to explain complex concepts, and provided examples to illustrate the practical applications of the findings. I also encouraged questions and feedback to ensure my message was understood, and adjusted my communication strategy accordingly.

history.question: Tell me about a time when you had to explain a complex technical concept, such as a machine learning model or a software development project, to a non-technical team member or stakeholder. How did you approach the explanation, and what strategies did you use to ensure your message was understood?
history.feedback: The candidate failed to provide a relevant example or any insight into their experience with explaining complex technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders. This lack of response indicates a potential gap in communication skills, a crucial aspect of working in a team environment.
history.critique: The answer does not address the question, showing a lack of preparation or experience in explaining technical concepts to non-technical audiences.
history.rewrite: In my previous role, I had to explain a machine learning model to a marketing team. I approached this by first understanding their level of technical knowledge and then using analogies and visual aids to simplify the concept. I ensured my message was understood by asking for feedback and adjusting my explanation accordingly.

history.question: Tell me about a time when you had to explain a technical concept, such as a data structure or an algorithm, to a non-technical team member or stakeholder. How did you approach the explanation, and what strategies did you use to ensure your message was understood?
history.feedback: The candidate's response lacks a specific example and explanation of how they approached the situation, making it difficult to assess their ability to communicate technical concepts effectively.
history.critique: The candidate failed to provide a concrete example, and their response does not demonstrate any understanding of strategies for explaining technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders.
history.rewrite: In my previous role, I had to explain the concept of a hash table to a non-technical team member. I approached the explanation by using an analogy, comparing it to a phonebook where each name is associated with a phone number. I also used visual aids, such as diagrams, to help illustrate the concept. To ensure understanding, I asked questions throughout the explanation and provided examples of how hash tables are used in real-world applications.

history.question: Tell me about a time when you had to explain a complex technical concept, such as a machine learning model or a software development project, to a non-technical team member or stakeholder. How did you approach the explanation, and what strategies did you use to ensure your message was understood?
history.answer: In my Datathon, I explained a recommendation algorithm to a non-technical teammate by using simple analogies like “ranking preferences like sorting favorites.” I avoided jargon, used a quick visual flow, and tied it to business outcomes. I also paused to confirm understanding, which made the explanation clear and easy to follow.
history.feedback: The candidate provided a clear and concise example of explaining a complex technical concept to a non-technical team member. They effectively used analogies, avoided jargon, and incorporated visual aids to facilitate understanding. However, the answer could be improved by providing more specific details about the context and the outcome of the explanation.
history.critique: The answer is brief and lacks specific details about the Datathon and the teammate's response to the explanation.
history.rewrite: In my Datathon project, I had to explain a complex recommendation algorithm to a non-technical teammate who was responsible for presenting the results to stakeholders. I approached the explanation by using simple analogies, such as 'ranking preferences like sorting favorites,' and avoided using technical jargon. I also created a quick visual flow to illustrate how the algorithm worked and tied it to the business outcomes we were trying to achieve. To ensure understanding, I paused to confirm my teammate's comprehension and asked for feedback. As a result, my teammate was able to effectively communicate the results to the stakeholders, and we were able to implement the algorithm successfully.
